coredata.dc:title: Doping of carbon nanodots for saving cells from silver nanotoxicity: A study on recovering osteogenic differentiation potential
coredata.dc:creator: Das, Bodhisatwa
Dadhich, Prabhash
Pal, Pallabi
Dutta, Joy
Srivas, Pavan Kumar
Dutta, Abir
Mohapatra, Pradeep Kumar Das
Maity, Ananda Mukul
Bera, Sutanuka
Dhara, Santanu
coredata.dc:description: Silver nanoparticles are explored for many advanced biological applications including the development of antimicrobial surfaces on implants, SERS imaging, nanotherapeutics, biosensing and much more. However, recent research findings suggest silver nanoparticles provide blockade of differentiation of mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs), especially into osteogenic developmental pathway via generation of reactive oxygen species. These studies suggest that the application of silver nanoparticles in medical implants should be prohibited. In the current study, carbon nanodots (CND) supported silver clusters (AgC) is explored as a remedy to this problem. The nanostructure was synthesized in microwave irradiation induced rapid method and characterization was conducted via UV–Vis spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, HRTEM, XRD, FTIR, Raman spectroscopy, DLS, AFM, and XPS. Fluorescence spectrum showed a quantum yield of 0.25 while Raman spectroscopy showed rapid amplification of CND specific peaks implicating significant SERS property. Further in vitro biocompatibility (MTT) and bio-imaging capability was assessed culturing Wharton's Jelly-derived MSCs. In this study, its efficacy as in-situ cellular oxidative stress scavenger is also studied using NBT and DCFH-DA assay. Via ALP assay, alizarin red staining, cell membrane nanoindentation studies, PCR analysis and immunocytochemistry for osteoblast-like gene expression it was confirmed that AgCs can control silver nanoparticle-induced inhibition of osteogenic differentiation in vitro. Thus, AgCs (Carbon nanodots supported silver clusters) are not only considered to be a dual-mode bio-imaging nanoprobe but also a remedy to the silver-induced ROS generation and osteogenic differentiation blockade of MSCs.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cell viability, ROS scavenging activity and in vitro bio-imaging study

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis protocol of AgCs synthesis and AgCs under white light and UV Light.
a) UV Vis spectra of AgCs; b) Fluorescence spectra of AgCs; c) Raman spctra of AgCs; d) FTIR spectra of AgCs.
a) TEM image of AgCs (inset high resolution image); b) AFM image of AgCs; c) XRD analysis of AgCs; d) DLS analysis of AgCs.
XPS Analysis of AgCs a) Survey scan; b) Ag3d; c) O1s; d) C1s.
a) Plates showing ZOI of AgCs against E. coli and S.aureus. b) Schematic comparison of ZOI diameter (* denotes p ≤ .05 considered to be statistically significant, n = 3).
a) MTT assay results of AgCs (24 h and 72 h, CN, control); b) NBT Assay results of AgCs; c) DCFH-DA assay of AgCs (* denotes p ≤ .05 considered to be statistically significant).
Bioimaging of MSCs via Fluorescence Microscopy a) AgCs; b) DAPI; c) bright field; d) merged.

Blood compatibility Test a)Hemolysis assay; b) RBC aggregation control; c) RBC aggregation AgC (10 μg/mL); d) RBC aggregation AgC(20 μg/mL); e) WBC aggregation control; f) WBC aggregation AgC (10 μg/mL); g) WBC aggregation AgC (20 μg/mL); h) platelet aggregation control; i) platelet aggregation AgC (10 μg/mL); j) platelet aggregation AgC (20 μg/mL).

a) MSCs with AgCs alizarin red stained; b) MSCs control alizarin red stained; c) ALP assay analysis for AgCs; d) Nanoindentation study of the cell membrane (* denotes p ≤ .05 considered to be statistically significant). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
PCR Results of a) ALP; b) Col-I; c) Osteocalcin; d) GAPDH expression of AgC treated MSCs with respect to control (* denotes p ≤ .05 considered to be statistically significant).
Immunocytochemistry results: a) Osteocalcin AgCs; b) Osteocalcin-control; c) Run X-2 AgCs; d) RunX-2 control; e) Col-I AgCs; f) Col-I control.
Proposed mechanism of self ROS scavenging of AgCs.

head.ce:title: Doping of carbon nanodots for saving cells from silver nanotoxicity: A study on recovering osteogenic differentiation potential

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biomedical Engineering, Rutgers The State University of New Jersey, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Rutgers The State University of New Jersey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Medical Science and Technology, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Medical Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Technology Development Centre, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Microbiology, Vidyasagar University, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: B. Das, Biomedical Engineering, Rutgers The State University of New Jersey, USA.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Rutgers The State University of New Jersey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Silver nanoparticles are explored for many advanced biological applications including the development of antimicrobial surfaces on implants, SERS imaging, nanotherapeutics, biosensing and much more. However, recent research findings suggest silver nanoparticles provide blockade of differentiation of mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs), especially into osteogenic developmental pathway via generation of reactive oxygen species. These studies suggest that the application of silver nanoparticles in medical implants should be prohibited. In the current study, carbon nanodots (CND) supported silver clusters (AgC) is explored as a remedy to this problem. The nanostructure was synthesized in microwave irradiation induced rapid method and characterization was conducted via UV–Vis spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, HRTEM, XRD, FTIR, Raman spectroscopy, DLS, AFM, and XPS. Fluorescence spectrum showed a quantum yield of 0.25 while Raman spectroscopy showed rapid amplification of CND specific peaks implicating significant SERS property. Further in vitro biocompatibility (MTT) and bio-imaging capability was assessed culturing Wharton's Jelly-derived MSCs. In this study, its efficacy as in-situ cellular oxidative stress scavenger is also studied using NBT and DCFH-DA assay. Via ALP assay, alizarin red staining, cell membrane nanoindentation studies, PCR analysis and immunocytochemistry for osteoblast-like gene expression it was confirmed that AgCs can control silver nanoparticle-induced inhibition of osteogenic differentiation in vitro. Thus, AgCs (Carbon nanodots supported silver clusters) are not only considered to be a dual-mode bio-imaging nanoprobe but also a remedy to the silver-induced ROS generation and osteogenic differentiation blockade of MSCs.

ce:section.ce:para: Multidrug and antibiotic resistant bacteria are one of the major challenges in current edge infection biology. With the random application of antibiotics and differential contact of bacteria and usual drugs in pathogenic environments like hospitals has emerged the current microbiology research into a new endeavor with drug-resistant pathogens inclusive “super bugs”. This infection control scenario becomes even more challenging in the case of the wound and post-implantation secondary infection management (Godoy-Gallardo et al., 2014; Thompson et al., 2015). Most of the cases, these infections occur due to multiple bacterial infections and most of them are drug-resistant. The problem lies more with the orthopedic implantations as infection can cause from an implant failure to a morbid revision surgery. It is reported that almost 5% of the total orthopedic devices implanted fails mostly due to infections (Trampuz and Widmer, 2006; Darouiche, 2004).

To cope up with orthopedic implants and scaffolds being failed against infection, incorporation of antibiotic or bacteriostatic materials in implants are common practices. There are several reports of antibiotic coatings, antimicrobial bio-ceramic spraying and controlled releasing of antibiotics from metallic implants like titanium and stainless steel (Bezuidenhout et al., 2015). However, the problem with multidrug-resistant bacteria persists and multiple research groups are working in this challenging area (Stigter et al., 2004; Kazemzadeh-Narbat et al., 2013; He et al., 2014).

In that context, nanosilver and silver ions are observed to be significantly effective against many of the antibiotic-resistant pathogens (Lara et al., 2010; Anisha et al., 2013). The silver nanoparticles are easy to synthesize and apply upon the graft, implant surface, and bulk. There are several approaches for incorporation of silver nanoparticles to titanium surfaces. This includes plasma implantation of silver nanoparticles to implant surfaces electrochemical plating or deposition and incorporation of silver nanoparticles into bioceramics and polymer coatings via surface functionalization (Qin et al., 2014; Qiao et al., 2015; Necula et al., 2012; Ma et al., 2011; Zhang et al., 2013; Kolmas et al., 2014). Not only in the form of the coating as implants but for tissue engineering scaffolds, dental filler, bone cement, wound healing materials and many other biomedical material related applications have been reported in literature utilizing nanosilver for gaining asepsis(Mohamed Hamouda, 2012; Murphy et al., 2015; Guo et al., 2013).

But each boon has its own limitations and application-oriented hazards. There are significant reports of cytotoxicity of silver nanoparticles. Silver nanoparticles are observed to block cellular proliferation and growth in vitro systems (Loza et al., 2014a). There are significant reports on blocking of differentiation to the osteogenic lineage of stem cells due to exposure of nano silver (Sengstock et al., 2014; Albers et al., 2013). It is also reported to block growth factors like VEGF and IL-6 (Greulich et al., 2009). It has been observed that metal ions have effects on cytokine-activated pathways like NF-kβ and AP-1. Silver ions can generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) by which it allows nuclear translocation of the NF-kβ pathway. This causes differential secretion of cytokines (up-regulation of IL-6 or down-regulation of IL-8) (Sengstock et al., 2014).

Therefore, via generation of ROS, silver nanoparticles can affect differentiation of stem cells also. This limitation of the application of silver nanoparticles can lead to the hindrance of this material into medicinal products. As there are already significantly many reports in favor of the application of nanosilver related products, a solution to the ROS generation could be useful in mitigating this problem. In recent years carbon nanodots (CNDs) have been observed to be a novel tool for biomedical application (Zhu et al., 2013). It is not only useful for bioimaging but also observed to have scavenging properties for free radicals (Das et al., 2014; Das Purkayastha et al., 2014). Recently CNDs decorated silver nanoparticles have been observed to work as peroxidase equivalent and agent for photodynamic therapy (Jiang et al., 2014; Liu and Chen, 2013; Kleinauskas et al., 2013). In the current study, CND supported silver nanoclusters (AgCs) are examined for bioimaging, ROS scavenging, antimicrobial activity and SERS imaging capability. Further, the AgCs were checked for stem-cell differentiation blocking potential into osteogenic lineage.

All the materials utilized in the current study are used as it is without further purification if not mentioned. For all the reactions deionized water (DI water, conductivity: 18.5 mΩ cm−1) was utilized. AgNO3, Sucrose, Na2HPO4, KH2PO4, NaCl, KCl, NaOH etc. were procured from Merck India.

The synthesis was conducted in a two-step process. 50 mL of 1 M sucrose solution was prepared in DI water and its pH was raised up to 11 by adding 1 M NaOH solution. It was transferred to a domestic microwave (LG, 1200-W, 1 GHz) and irradiated for 5 min. The yellowish solution was cooled and kept at 4o C by icepack. 10 mL of 0.5 mM AgNO3 solution was added dropwise along with vigorous stirring. In the current study, CNDs were used both as a reducing agent as well as a stabilizer for silver nanoclusters. The collected sample was filtered through a 0.2 μm syringe filter (BD Biosciences). The pH was adjusted to 7 by adding 1 M HCl solution. The excess salt was removed via dialysis. The final solution was freeze dried and a suspension of 100 μg/mL was prepared as stock for further experiments in PBS (phosphate buffered saline).

The synthesized AgCs were characterized with UV–Vis spectroscopy (Varian Cary), fluorescence spectroscopy (Perkin Elmer LS55), XPS (VG scientific ESCALAB MK-2, Mg Kα), HRTEM (JEOL, JEM-2100), EDAX (Oxford Instruments), FTIR (Thermo Nicolet, Nexus 8700), Raman spectroscopy (Jobin Yvon Horiba, T 64000), DLS (Malvern, Zetasizer), SEM (Carl Zeiss, EVO 60), AFM (Agilent 5500). The fluorescence quantum yield was measured with respect to 1 (M) quinine sulfate.

The antimicrobial activity of AgCs was measured against S.aureus and E.Coli (Gram +ve and -ve) for applicability of the material in a biomedical composite. 100 μg/mL stock AgC solutions were prepared and diluted to 1:10 and 1:100 dilutions. Whatman filter paper (BD-Biosciences) was cut into 8 mm circular discs and dip coated into these 3 concentrations. For control standard antibiotic-antimycotic solution (Gibco, concentration 100×: w/ 10,000 U Penicillin, 10 mg Streptomycin and 25 μg Amphotericin B per ml in 0.9% normal saline Sterile filtered) was utilized. The antimicrobial activity was measured by measuring zone of inhibition in agar plates. The bacterial spore content for S. aureus and E. coli were 1.47 ∗ 107 and 1.1 ∗ 107 CFU mL−1, respectively. The activity was measured in replica plates (n = 3).

The study was conducted via using Wharton's Jelly-derived Mesenchymal Stem Cells (MSCs) isolated under Institute Human Ethical Clearance Committee of Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur, and Midnapore Medical College, Midnapore, West Bengal, India.

The cells were cultured in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM-high glucose) with 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% antibiotic (Gibco, Thermo Fischer). 3-[4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl]-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay was conducted according to the protocol described by Mosmann (1983). The MTT assay was used to measure the mitochondrial activity, which is correlated to cell viability for both attached and poorly attached cells. Metabolically active cells can reduce the MTT tetrazolium salt to formazan crystals, while dead cells do not. Cells were grown on cell culture plates, with an initial seeding density of 104 cells per well, and the samples were added along with the media. The cells were incubated for 72 h. The culture media was discarded and the MTT salt solution (Invitrogen) was added to a concentration of 5 mg mL−1. The samples were incubated for 4 h. Finally, the cells were lysed by DMSO and the optical density was measured at 590 nm.

The bio-imaging study was conducted by growing cells on poly-l-lysine coated coverslips (Blue-Star) (104 cells/sample). The AgC mixed media was added (5 μg/mL) to the cells and incubated for 12 h. The media was removed, and the coverslips were washed with PBS. The cells were counterstained with DAPI (Life Technologies) for nucleus and imaged using an inverted fluorescence microscope (Carl Zeiss, Germany). The coverslips were mounted on glass slides (Hi-Media) and imaged using both fluorescence as well as the DIC (differential interference contrast) mode. Two filters of excitation (365 nm bandpass 25 nm, and 470 nm bandpass 50 nm) and emission (450 nm bandpass 25 nm, and 540 nm bandpass 25 nm) were employed for capturing the fluorescence micrographs. All the micrographs were captured at 20× magnification.

Hemolysis assay was conducted in view of the material to be applicable to in vivo systems, like the optical imaging of blood vessels and blood-related sensing assays. Briefly, uncoagulated blood (3% citrate) was mixed with an equal volume of sterile normal saline. Different amounts of samples were mixed with 1 mL of saline mixed blood and incubated for 60 min. Subsequently, the samples were centrifuged, and the optical densities were measured at 540 nm. Negative and positive controls were taken as normal saline and 1% Triton-X solution.

For the blood cell aggregation study, 5 mL of blood was collected from the subject (mixed with 3% Na-citrate 100 μL/mL of blood) and subdivided into two parts for RBC aggregation and for WBC and platelets adhesion studies. For the RBC aggregation assay, 2 mL blood was centrifuged at 700 rpm for 10 min. The pellet was collected, and the pellet was mixed with normal saline in 1:9 volume ratios. From that solution, 100 μL was taken and mixed with 600 μL of normal saline in the test tubes for each sample. The rest of the 1 mL tube was completed by adding the sample and saline to make up the volume up to 1 mL. The sample was incubated for 60 min, and afterward, 1 drop of the resultant solution was placed on a glass slide and observed under a microscope. For platelet and WBC aggregation assays, 4 mL uncoagulated blood was mixed with an equal volume of saline. This solution was mixed with HiSep™LSM-1077 (HiMedia) in a volume ratio of 3:4. This mixture was centrifuged at 40g for 30 min. The mixture during this treatment was separated into three distinct layers of platelet rich plasma, WBC, and RBC. The platelet rich plasma and WBC layers were isolated and mixed with the sample in a 9: 1 volume ratio. After incubation of 60 min, a drop was placed on a glass slide and observed under a microscope.

The AgCs synthesized are observed not only have imaging application but also therapeutic potential. The CNDs are observed to be capable of scavenging free radicals from cells. The in vitro hydroxyl free radical scavenging property of the AgCs was verified via Di-Chloro Di-Hydrofuran Fluorescein Di-Acetate (DCFH-DA) assay. Briefly, 104 mL−1 cells per well were cultured on a poly-l-lysine-coated 12 well cell culture plate. Different concentrations of AgCs (10–30 μg/mL) were added to the wells After 72 h of culture, the culture medium was discarded and washed with sterile PBS. The cells were incubated with 1 mM methanolic DCFH-DA solution (Sigma-Aldrich) for 1 h and the fluorescence intensity was measured by excitation at 485 nm and emission at 530 nm. The inhibition of hydroxyl radical was measured with respect to the controls (without samples).

NBT (Nitro Blue Tetrazolium) assays in vitro both by spectroscopic and microscopic methods were performed by using the protocol described previously elsewhere, but with certain modifications (Sim Choi et al., 2006). Briefly, 6 mg mL−1 NBT (SRL Chemicals, Mumbai) solution was prepared in PBS and protected from light. MSCs cultured in DMEM (Hi Media) were added in poly-l-lysine coated culture plates along with different concentrations of sample. For spectroscopy, the media was discarded, and the cells were lysed to dissolve the formed crystals using DMSO and 1 M KOH solution (250 μL each). Finally, the optical density was measured at 610 nm.

MSCs were seeded in tissue culture well-plates containing the standard media and the AgC suspensions were tested. After 12 h, the media was discarded, and fresh osteogenic media (HiMedia) containing the agents were added. ALP activity was measured post 7d of culturing. After washing the cells thrice with PBS, they were incubated with 15 mM p-nitrophenyl phosphate for 20 min at 37 °C in alkaline phosphatase buffer. The reaction was stopped by adding 1 M NaOH and absorbance was measured at 405 nm on Platescreen (RMS health care system, India).

Calcium deposition is one of the major markers for osteogenic differentiation. So, MSCs were seeded on poly-l-lysine coated coverslips and allowed to grow in osteogenic media (HiMedia) in presence of AgCs for 15 d with a change in media and addition of AgCs in every third day. After 15 days the media above the coverslips were discarded and washed thrice with PBS. The cover slips were fixed using 4% paraformaldehyde for 20 min. The alizarin red staining was conducted using a working solution of 2% (Sigma-Aldrich) for 1 min.

The osteoblastic differentiation of MSCs is often characterized by calcium deposition and enhancement of the membrane stiffness. To check the differentiation capability of the samples the cells were grown on polylysine coated coverslips and supplemented with osteogenic media (HiMedia). Three different type of samples were analyzed (n = 3) like control, 0.1% AgCs and 0.1% AgNO3 supplemented. The cell culture was continued in osteogenic media for two weeks. The cellular membranes' mechanical property was measured in nanoindentation system TI 950 TriboIndenter, Hysitron Inc., USA. 20 different spots were analyzed for reporting the mean hardness and standard deviation.

The gene expression down-regulation in presence of AgCs was studied via RT-PCR studies. MSCs were seeded in the six-well plates and allowed to grow in osteogenic media in presence of AgCs. RNA was isolated after 15d of culture and different gene expression was studied using a kit-based protocol provided by Thermo-Fischer. RNA concentration was normalized via measurement in Nanodrop™. GAPDH was kept as house-keeping gene and its expression was checked to similar. The genes' expressions which were considered into account are ALP, osteocalcin, and collagen-I. For CDNA synthesis and gene expression checking kits-based protocol and reagents provided by Thermo-Fischer was utilized in a Thermocycler (Biorad systems) machine. The gene expressions were checked using 1% Agarose gel (ethidium bromide as fluorescence agent) and imaged in Gel-Doc machine (Dolphin Doc plus) machine. The images were analyzed using ImageJ freeware.
Unlabelled Table
Gene Forward primer Reverse primer Melting temperature
Collagen-I GCGCCAGAAGAACTGGTAGCAA GCGCGCCATACTCGAACTGGAA 66.2
Osteocalcin CCTTTGTGTCCAAGCAGGA CTAGACCGGGCCGTAGAA 57.5
ALP ATGGGATGGGTGTCTCCACA CCACGAAGGGGAACTTGTC 61
GAPDH CCATGGAGAAGGCTGGGG CAAAGTTGTCATGGATGACC 54

The protein expression was carried out to check the differentiation of the cells in proteomic level. The cells after growing in osteogenic media in presence or absence of AgCs were fixed using 4% paraformaldehyde followed by PBS washing, 0.1% TritonX-100, and 1% BSA treatment. After final PBS wash, the samples were treated with primary antibody (Osteocalcin, RunX-2, and Col-I, Abcam) and incubated at 4o C overnight. It was washed several times for nonspecific binding removal followed by staining with fluorescent secondary antibody (FITC Conjugated Life Technologies) and counterstained with DAPI and imaged under a fluorescence microscope (Carl Zeiss).

All experiments were performed in triplicates (n = 3). Data were expressed in mean ± standard deviation. Statistical analysis was carried out using Student's t-test and a p-value of 0.05 was significant.

AgCs were synthesized in a simple microwave-assisted method. The carbon precursor was initially irradiated in a domestic microwave to develop the CND suspension. Post microwave irradiation it turns from colorless to yellowish. This sample was ice chilled and (0.5 mM) AgNO3 solution was added dropwise along with vigorous stirring. After few drops of addition, the sample tends to turn brownish. The suspension was passed through a 0.2 μm syringe for filtration and final suspension was freeze-dried. Finally, a suspension of 100 μg mL−1 was prepared. The brownish solution under UV light was observed to emit bright green fluorescence (Fig. 1 ).

The possible scheme of synthesis of AgCs via reduction and stabilization was also shown. Previous reports suggest the presence of OH groups on the CND surface which can act as a proton donor with the reduction of silver ions into atomic silver which is immediately stabilized as silver clusters in CND structures itself.

The physicochemical analysis involves several characterizations to observe many properties for the synthesized AgCs. The UV–Vis spectra for AgCs show certain specific signatures which depict the presence of carbon nanostructures and silver nanostructures (Fig. 2a). Basically, the major feature for Ag-NPs we can observe is the peak due to surface plasmon resonance (SPR) at around 400 nm. Due to delocalization of p-p⁎ and n-p⁎electronic bands two more features are observed near 340 nm and 370 nm in the UV–Vis spectra. There is a shift of these two bands mostly due to doping of silver atoms in between. The fluorescence spectrum is important for understanding the bioimaging capability of the AgCs.

In the current study, two emission spectra of the AgCs are reported due to excitation two different wavelengths (400 and 430 nm). With the shift in the excitation wavelength, the emission peaks are shifted equally (Fig. 2b). 3 distinct peaks are observed in both the spectra. It is already evidenced in the literature that silver clusters are also quantum confined structures with significant photoluminescence property. Due to the nearby occurrence of multiple luminescent species in the same nanostructure (Ag-clusters and CNDs), therefore the possibility of FRET (fluorescence resonance energy transfer) is significant. The quantum yield with respect to quinine sulfate was observed to be around 25%. As we understand CNDs are the bulk phase and Ag-clusters are present as islands being the dopant. The most left positioned peak is mostly due to silver clusters as the smallest entity. Therefore, mostly Ag-clusters behave as the donor in this procedure. The other two peaks, especially near the NIR region peak is mostly due to CNDs. An important feature of silver nanostructures for which they are very much useful in sensing application is surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) in which intensity of Raman band of a functional group/ chemical species is enhanced drastically. This specific feature is observed in the Raman spectra of AgCs (Fig. 2c). In that Figure, two major bands characteristics of CNDs D and G band are observed around 1200 and 1600 cm−1. Some other features observed in the area of 1400 cm−1 and 1800 cm−1 due to the formation of CC and CH linkages and CO side chains. Most of these bands are again significantly enhanced when the spectra of AgCs were carried out (red line). However, due to the presence of a stronger electron acceptor (Ag atoms) nearby to the carbon atoms in AgCs, the bands are shifted in the Raman spectra of AgCs. This intensity amplification proves that AgCs can be useful for SERS functional imaging and trafficking study via bio-conjugation and multimodal imaging.

In the FTIR spectra of the AgCs (Fig. 2d), mostly the features of the side groups of CNDs are observed. This includes the peaks for surface-bound OH (3400), CO (1700–1900), CC (2500), CN (1000−1100), NH2 (1600). However, in the spectra of the AgCs had certain extra features mostly due to the presence of Ag atoms near carbon structures which cause changes in the molecular electronic microenvironment.

The major properties of AgCs were also measured via transmission electron microscopy (TEM). In the TEM image, it is observed that AgCs are mostly spherical in shape and sized mostly 8–12 nm. The crystal lattices are measured and observed that all of them are to be from silver i.e. the carbon structures formed are mostly amorphous in nature. The crystal plane distance was measured and matched with corresponding JCPDS file (41–1402, silver cubic). It matches with two crystal planes lattice parameters [112] and [011].

The Z profile of AgCs was measured by using AFM analysis (Fig. 3b). Except for a few larger aggregates, most of the particle dimension was observed to be within 3–7 nm which depicts a tendency of AgCs to spherical to plate-like shape. The results obtained in TEM were verified in the XRD analysis. Many of the angles for JCPDS file (41–1402) were observed to be present in the XRD analysis also (Fig. 3c). It includes angles like 35.89, 37.04, 40.30, 45.305, 51.68, 55.07, 64.63, 76.8, 82.35 etc. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) determines hydrodynamic radius of a material. It determines the size with which a nanoparticle interacts in a suspension. The DLS results of AgCs shows a size range mostly belonging to 20–30 nm which is in accordance with TEM results (Fig. 3d). However, certain large aggregates also exist in the suspension.

The chemical bonding and conjugation of a material are better interpreted from XPS analysis (Fig. 4 ). The AgCs XPS scan shows peaks for silver, carbon, oxygen and also some impurities mostly due to the presence of some salts which remained nominally as impurities after dialysis.

As we observe Ag-3d spectra in Fig. 4(b), most of the peak designated are for metallic silver 374.2 (3d3), 371 (3d) and 366.8 (2 s) ev respectively. However, a small notch around 368.2 ev denotes partial oxidation of the silver atoms. However, no peak for AgC bond formation is observed which denotes formation of the intact silver cluster in between CND lattice. This is confirmed by an O1s peak at 529.4 ev (Fig. 4c). However, when we look at spectra of C1s, at 283 ev a strong peak for metal carbide (doping of silver in carbon lattices), 284.8 ev notch for CC graphitic linkage, 285 ev notch for C1s and CO linkages at 286 ev is observed. Therefore, we can interpret that conjugation of silver to carbon could be of both AgC and Ag-O-C linkages. More importantly, there are also presence of CO (287.8) and CO linkages which are accordance with FTIR results and could be useful for further bioconjugation.

The major reason for the current study is to develop a material which can provide antimicrobial activity without hampering cellular activity by the production of ROS. So, antimicrobial activity against both S. aureus and E. coli (Gram +ve and Gram −ve models) strains was examined via measuring the zone of inhibitions.

In this study, the ZOI was compared against a standard anti microbial/ antifungal solution (Gibco, 10,000 units/mL of penicillin, 10,000 μg/mL of streptomycin, and 25 μg/mL Amphotericin B). Three different concentrations of AgCs were utilized for ZOI study. All the concentration showed antimicrobial activity (Fig. 5 ). However, all of them had lower antimicrobial activity than standard (maximum 75% against E. coli and 50% against S. aureus). However, antibiotic solutions work as burst release molecule. In case of AgCs, ions are released in a controlled way which could be depicted as the major advantage of the application of nanoparticles. Moreover, the antibiotic utilized is of 100× concentrations suggesting significant antimicrobial properties of AgCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell viability, ROS scavenging activity and in vitro bio-imaging study
ce:section.ce:para: The cytocompatibility of the AgCs is significantly important for its applicability in a cellular system. The cytocompatibility was measured in two instants, first in 24 h and again in 72 h to compare the immediate toxicity and chronic toxicity. The MTT assay results show at initial phase a sharp decrease in the cellular viability (Fig. 6a). None of the sample concentrations reached >60% viability which is required for the biological applications. However, after 72 h, a drastic change in the scenario is observed. Almost all the samples not only reach >90% viability, in some concentrations higher cell viability than the control is observed.

The major reason of CNDs substitution with silver clusters is its capability to scavenge ROS. Therefore, NBT assay was conducted to check the superoxide scavenging property (Fig. 6b). It was observed that in vitro model of ROS generation (incubation in UV light), cause more superoxides generation in a control sample (no AgCs) rather than the samples containing AgCs. The sample containing AgCs (30% vol/vol) was observed to be capable of almost scavenging 70% of the superoxide generated (the results are observed to be statistically significant).

However, when in vitro hydroxyl radical inhibition activity was measured via DCFH-DA assay in the same method, the hydroxyl radical scavenging by AgCs was not observed to be as effective as NBT assay (Fig. 6c). Even though at higher concentration the scavenging was significantly effective, but at a lower concentration only 20% scavenging was possible. A major advantage of the application of AgCs is it is fluorescent and can be useful for bioimaging. MSCs labeling study was conducted to check its bioimaging capability. It has been observed that the cells are uniformly labeled by the AgCs (Fig. 7 ). AgCs were observed to be distributed in the cytoplasm as well as in the nucleus of the cells.

As AgCs is having a potential application in biomedical implants and tissue engineering grafts for in vivo applications one more hurdle this material was tested for. The material was subjected to the blood-compatibility test. Two major tests RBC lysis and blood cell aggregation were tested (Fig. 8 ). Importantly hemolysis is the first test in which if failed an implant is immediately rejected. In Fig. 8a) it is observed that with respect to the Triton-X solution, AgCs to provide a nominal hemolytic activity which is much below the risk boundary (<5%).

The blood cell aggregation study is important mostly because in presence of nanoparticles the surface charge of blood cells tend to change. Their adherence can cause immense health hazards like immune response (for WBCs) and activation of blood coagulation cascade (for platelets adhesion due to change in surface potential). However, in the above Figures (Fig. 8b and j) no specific tendency of cellular aggregation is noticed in AgC applied samples with respect to control. However, in certain cases aggregation of RBCs is observed which is mostly due to osmotic shocks as the effect of osmotic shock is highly predominant on RBCs.

As any osteogenic implant's or grafts' one major quality is bone tissue in-growth and MSCs differentiation capability towards the bone formation. Therefore, ALP assay and alizarin red study are conducted with MSCs cultured in osteogenic medium to check whether AgCs prohibit MSCs differentiation into osteogenic lineage (Fig. 9 ). ALP assay is useful to understand cellular phosphate metabolism. It is an early marker of osteogenic differentiation. On the other hand, the alizarin red stain is useful for determining the deposition of calcium into the cells which are a marker for osteogenesis.

It is observed that in presence of AgCs number of reddish-black spots (calcium deposits) is less than control even though same culture condition and osteogenic media was supplemented (Fig. 9a and b). But the number of cells is more prevalent in AgCs and calcium deposition points are not so much less that it could be considered as differentiation blocking. A similar trend is also followed in ALP assay (Fig. 9c). The levels of ALP activity in AgC containing samples are less than control but in the range of 80–90% of the control. The mechanical characterization of the differentiated cells was carried out using nanoindentation study and hardness of the cells was considered as a marker of osteogenic differentiation as during this differentiation process mineralization of the cells and nearby matrix takes place which causes hardening of the tissue. Bare silver ions, AgCs, and cells without any treatment were grown in osteogenic media. The results were significantly noticeable after two weeks culture. This trend is similar to previous reports (Miyamoto et al., 2015). The observed results were reported in (Fig. 9d). The hardness value obtained from control is around 180 MPa for control, 105 MPa for AgCs and around 50 MPa for bare Ag ions. Therefore, doping into CND has allowed the osteogenic differentiation significantly and enhanced mineralization proved by almost two-fold increased mechanical property with respect to bare silver ions.

The osteogenic differentiation of MSCs in presence of AgCs is possible is further verified via in vitro gene expression study. The markers used to check this expression possibility used are Col-I, ALP, Osteocalcin. Also, GAPDH was used as a housekeeping gene (Fig. 10 ).

The PCR results suggest there is a decrease in most of the gene expression in samples with respect to control (Fig. 10d). Even then, osteocalcin and Col-I expression showed an increase in gene expression with AgCs. Importantly, expression of osteocalcin is observed to be exceptionally good in presence of AgCs. ALP expression observed to show almost no difference between gene expression of control and sample. However, ALP being an early marker for osteogenesis its expression can be negligible. For other two osteogenic genes we find a similar expression pattern which suggest that up to a concentration, doping into carbon dots help in saving the cells beyond which they do not.

To check further in details three major proteins expressed during MSCs differentiating into osteogenesis were targeted for antibody staining. Osteocalcin is mostly expressed in the cellular cytoplasm. It has been observed to be expressed almost equally in both sample and control (Fig. 11a and b). However, the expression in AgCs observed is lower than the control (no silver only osteogenic media). The similar trend also was observed in nuclear protein RunX-2. But in terms of Col-I expression, the sample showed much poorer performance with respect to control. Osteocalcin being one of the major markers of the osteogenic pathway, looking at its expression pattern it can be concluded that effectively silver doping into CNDs enhanced the osteogenic potential.

Silver nanoparticles are observed to be a novel alternative against antibiotic-resistant microbes and highly useful for application in implants and grafts for protection against secondary infection and hospital-borne infection (which are mostly drug-resistant) (Zhao et al., 2011; Furno et al., 2004; Jacob Inbaneson et al., 2011; Ansari et al., 2015; Mühling et al., 2009). However, its toxic effect limits its biomedical application. There are several reports of neurotoxicity, developmental defects, proliferation and differentiation blocking potential of silver nanoparticles (Ong et al., 2016; Wu et al., 2015). In vitro and in vivo studies suggest there are multiple variances of the toxicological properties due to size, shape, and coating shell of the silver nanostructures (Nguyen et al., 2016; Powers et al., 2011). There are multiple theories regarding the methods via which silver nanoparticles affect the cellular system. Many researchers suggest genotoxicity, chromosomal aberration, and DNA damage is the major concern regarding silver nanotoxicity. Silver nanoparticles are also reported to cause degradation to membrane proteins and lipids (Bao et al., 2015; Patlolla et al., 2015; El Mahdy et al., 2015; Huang et al., 2015; Lesniak et al., 2013). A recent report suggests silver nanoparticles can attach to the cellular membrane, get internalized via VE-cadherin and affect endothelial functions (Sun et al., 2016). Another major concern for application of silver nanoparticles in cell biology lies as the due to the overlap of the dose effective for antimicrobial activity and biological toxicology limits (Greulich et al., 2012). While there are multiple theories involve in the effect of silver nanoparticles toxicity, one single cause for all of these effects is common and that is the generation of ROS. Silver nanoparticles and silver ions develop ROS which attacks DNA, membrane proteins and lipids causing mutation, peroxidation, and apoptosis (McCracken et al., 2015; Ahlberg et al., 2016; Onodera et al., 2015; McShan et al., 2014). It has been observed that electron accepting capping material such as citrate reduces cytotoxicity of nanosilver (Flores et al., 2013). It is also previously reported that silver nanostructures stabilized in carbon moiety can help in oxygen reduction, dye reduction etc. (Liu and Chen, 2013; Jin et al., 2015; Jana et al., 2015). Our previous study suggests CNDs can save cells from phototoxicity also (Das et al., 2016). The MTT assay result denotes long-term bioactive nature of the AgCs. There are also previous reports for carbon dots to be bioactive in nature (Kumar et al., 2017). Considering all these facts in the current study we propose Ag nanoclusters supported on carbon dots to be a novel alternative to combat the ROS generation property of silver nanoparticles. The carbon dots can scavenge and control its ROS generation which can effectively reduce the cellular toxicity. The DCFH-DA and NBT results suggest a reduction of ROS generation is achieved via doping of CNDs. Further nanoindentation and gene expression studies strongly enforce the possible differentiation potential of MSCs and decreased cytotoxicity of silver ions in presence of carbon dots. In vivo studies also depict effective mineralization and ectopic bony nodules formation which is comparable to the control study (Supplementary).

It was first reported in the year 2014 that there is an effect of silver nanoparticles towards blocking osteogenic differentiation of MSCs (Sengstock et al., 2014). They mostly correlated this effect with the generation of ROS and affecting the inflammatory pathways and cytokines associated with the osteoblast differentiation of MSCs. However, they were not clear about the osteogenic blocking mechanism (Loza et al., 2014b). Multiple reports published later on showed in the biological system Ag atoms forms Ag+ ions via reacting with ROS such as H2O2 and further reacts with biological buffer system to react with ions such as chlorides to deposit as salts AgCl. This destabilizes the pH balance generating excess H+ ions and creates acidic tissue microenvironment (Tao et al., 2016). Zheng et al. (2016) reported a rapid decrease of osteogenic potential in low pH due to proton-sensing GPR4-mediated suppression of Yes-associated protein (YAPs) (Zheng et al., 2016). This mechanism often leads to osteomyelitis and rapid bone decay in vivo. As in presence of silver nanoparticles, there is a local depletion of pH of tissue, this effect could attribute to osteogenic differentiation block. In the same report (Sengstock et al.) shown that uptake of silver nanoparticles does not affect ALP activity at all. Therefore, the effect of AgC might not be concentration-dependent in the case of ALP assay as observed in the results.

The doping of silver clusters into CNDs could be considered as novel means for controlled generation of ROS for antimicrobial activity. CNDs have been previously reported to function as an ROS scavenger via accepting singlet electrons of ROS into their carbon backbone and release them into cellular microenvironment as carbonate and bicarbonate buffer (Das et al., 2014). It can also release sulfate and phosphate into the cellular microenvironment by the similar mechanism (Das et al., 2016). Here also whenever ROS are generated from silver clusters, they are mostly taken up by CNDs itself. Only a few molecules could come out for showing antimicrobial activity while rest can be scavenged without harming the nearby healthy tissue. Further release of carbonate, bicarbonate and sulfate ions could react with the excessive H+ ions in the tissue microenvironment and restore the cellular pH by working as a buffer (Fig. 12 ). This also can restore osteogenesis and mineralization. Thus, doping of silver nanoclusters in carbon nanodots can save osteogenic differentiation process of stem cells via dual mechanisms as described above.

Therefore, the enhanced SERS peaks and fluorescence microscopy images establish AgCs to be utilizable as a multimodal imaging probe with self-phototoxicity reduction property. AgCs were also observed to have comparable antimicrobial activity also with respect to a very strong control antimicrobial agent (concentration 100×). Most importantly, the current study provides a novel application of carbon-based quantum sized materials in the application of reduction of nanotoxicity of other nanostructures via doping or making nanocomposites.

The results observed suggested that AgCs can quench the ROS generated by Ag-NCs. Ag-NPs has shown significant potential positive results against drug-resistant bacteria and hospital-acquired infections. So, to continue the application of AgNPs, doping of CNDs could be a good alternative for controlled release free radicals. Moreover, AgCs allow MSCs to differentiate and do not block any pathway. In vivo studies (supplementary) also show at the ectopic site AgCs treated MSCs cultured sample showed significant differentiation potential. Therefore, this material will be applicable for both coatings in orthopedic implants and bone tissue engineering grafts. Controlled release from implant surface, graft, and bone cement could be helpful not only for antimicrobial activity but for enhanced wound healing (due to ROS scavenging property). Not only for bone implants but this material could be used for wound dressing and grafts for chronic wounds such as diabetic foot ulcers where the generation of ROS destabilize the healing procedure (Vairamon et al., 2009; Finley et al., 2013). Further enhanced SERS and fluorescent bioimaging properties show the applicability of AgCs as a probe for dual-mode cellular imaging with auto ROS scavenging property. CND substitution hence could be the solution to ROS generation of most of the metal and metal oxide nanoparticles.
The authors declare that they do not have any conflict of interest in terms of financial source or intellectual properties
Ministry of Human Resources Development, Govt of India for the fellowships to authors.
